next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 3

The calibration bay ran at a constant seventeen degrees Celsius, two below facility standard. It wasn't for the athletes. It was for the equipment. Spectral analyzers drifted at higher temperatures, and Yusuf had spent three months arguing with Building Services for dedicated climate control. He'd won. The bay stayed cold, the analyzers stayed stable, and from October to April he wore a fleece under his lab coat.

He arrived at six forty-five, same time every morning. The processing stacks were already cycling, active calibration to standby analysis and back again, and their hum had become the sound of his working life. It ran at sixty hertz, low and steady. Most people stopped noticing it after a few minutes. Yusuf had stopped hearing it in his first week at Kirchberg, three years ago. Now he noticed it only when it changed.

He set his coffee on the workstation ledge, the same chipped mug with the Imperial College crest half gone, and opened the schedule. Six athletes before noon. Four after lunch. Then came data review and reporting. That was his official reporting. The other kind happened in the gaps.

The first athlete at seven was Anya Petrov, a javelin specialist from the Russian program. It was a standard monthly recalibration. Her right shoulder graft had been stable for two seasons, but the altitude altered the synthetic muscle's oxygen transfer and it needed periodic adjustment. She came in already stretching, one earbud still in, then pulled it free and dropped it in her lap as she settled into the rig.

The rig looked like a dentist's chair designe...

Evening dissociation: twenty minutes pass while Lena stares at a paragraph without reading it
prev_tail_excerpt:
...e Thursday for a follow-up. Get some sleep. Altitude takes about three days to stop messing with your deep cycles."

"Thanks."

"Yeah. Night, Brennan."

Kai walked back to his room through the fluorescent corridor. The facility was quiet now, nine-thirty, athletes in their quarters, the common areas emptied, the cafeteria dark. His footsteps were the only sound except the building's ventilation, thinner here than it had been by the calibration bay but steady enough to sand the edges off each step. He listened to his own gait on the hard floor: left foot, right foot, even and smooth, the synthetic grafts managing his stride with the consistency his natural legs had never quite achieved. He'd always been slightly dominant on the left side. Now he wasn't. The grafts had corrected the asymmetry without asking.

His room was dark except for the wall display's glow. Green readouts, all systems nominal, the system reporting on itself in the same quiet room where the person it was installed in was trying to fall asleep. He undressed, brushed his teeth, stood at the window. The mountains were invisible, just darkness and the faint reflection of the diagnostic display behind him, green numbers floating in the glass where the Bernese Alps should have been.

He thought about the graph. Two lines diverging. Forty to fifty milliseconds. A gap he hadn't been able to name until Yusuf drew it on a screen, and now it had a shape and a slope and the slope was up.

He reached for the light switch. The plastic met his fingertips while the decision was still forming, and then the room went dark.
